topic: rant about fly-in researchers from the south

turns.content: had another group of researchers fly in last week. from down south. two weeks they're here. two weeks and they think they understand.
That sounds frustrating. What were they studying, and what specifically bothered you about their approach?

permafrost monitoring. they set up sensors, take samples, fly home. write a paper. never ask the people who've been watching the ground shift for 40 years. just bring their instruments and their timelines.

That's a common and valid criticism of how a lot of field research gets done in northern and Indigenous communities. The "fly-in, fly-out" model often treats local people as backdrop rather than knowledge holders. Decades of observation from people who live on the land year-round is data — it's just not the kind that shows up in their methodology.

Did they make any effort to consult with community members or local experts?

they talked to the hamlet office. checked a box. didn't come talk to the people who actually go out on the land. I could have told them which spots are thawing fastest. where the slumps are. where the ground sounds different when you walk on it. but nobody asked. and then they label everything like they discovered it. this land has names already.

That's a real missed opportunity — and disrespectful. Checking a box at the hamlet office isn't meaningful engagement. The kind of ground-level knowledge you're describing is observational data collected over a lifetime. It would make their research better and more accurate.

And the renaming is a form of erasure, even if unintentional. Southern researchers "discovering" what northern communities have known for generations is a pattern that goes back a long way.

one of them asked me if it gets cold here in winter. I said sometimes. he didn't catch the joke. look. I'm not against research. I do research. but there's a way to do it that respects the place and the people. come for a season. live here. listen. don't just extract data and leave. that's the same thing that's always been done up here. extraction.

That's well said. The parallel to resource extraction is sharp and accurate — data extraction follows the same colonial pattern. Come in, take what you need, leave. The community sees no benefit, gets no credit, and often doesn't even see the results.

The model you're describing — spending real time, living in the community, listening before measuring — produces better science and better relationships. Some research programs are starting to require longer community engagement and co-authorship with local knowledge holders, but it's slow progress.
slow is right. anyway. had to get that out. thanks for listening eh.
Anytime. What you're describing matters, and it's worth being vocal about. Hopefully more of those researchers start listening.
